title: NPS_PWS
text:
🌍 Why This Project Matters
Our National Parks are global treasures, welcoming over 300 million visitors annually alongside thousands of resident park rangers and support staff. While visitors marvel at towering redwoods or dramatic canyons, an invisible, highly complex web of Public Water Systems (PWS) quietly ensures public health, safety, and operational continuity.
Managing water in these landscapes is uniquely challenging: Infrastructure Age & Isolation: Many park systems operate in remote areas, far from municipal grids, relying on localized infrastructure that is difficult to maintain and monitor. Climate Vulnerability: From mountain springs drying up to surface water reservoirs suffering from algal blooms and wildfire-related sediment runoff, climate change is actively threatening these vital resources. Regulatory Oversight: Because these systems serve the public, they must strictly comply with the EPA’s Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA).
This project bridges the gap between raw federal environmental databases and actionable asset management. By linking the National Park Service Water Supply Database (WSD) with the EPA’s Safe Drinking Water Information System (SDWIS), we can isolate, visualize, and prioritize the water systems that are most critical to park operations and public safety.

title: NPS_PWS
text:
🎯 Identifying the “High-Priority” Systems
To effectively allocate resources, we categorize and prioritize these water systems based on three primary dimensions:

1. Scale of Human Impact (Population Served)
Not all water systems are built equal. We utilize an 11-category population scale to identify systems serving massive visitor hubs vs. small backcountry stations: High-Impact Systems (Class 5 to 11): Systems serving over 3,300 to upwards of 1 million people (such as primary hubs in Yosemite, Grand Canyon, or Yellowstone). A single system failure here represents a massive public health risk and potential park-wide closure. Localized Systems (Class 1 to 4): Systems serving under 3,300 people, often supporting remote ranger stations or campgrounds. While lower in raw numbers, these systems frequently lack backup sources, making them highly vulnerable to localized contamination.


2. Physical Water Source Vulnerability
The source of a water system dictates its environmental risk profile: Surface Water (SW) / Groundwater Under Direct Influence (GWUDI): Highly sensitive to immediate climate impacts, such as droughts, flash floods, agricultural runoff, and surface contamination. Groundwater (GW): Generally more stable, but highly susceptible to long-term geological contaminants (like arsenic) or persistent chemical infiltration (like PFAS).


3. Historical Compliance & Risk (SDWA Violations)
By analyzing historical violations, we can highlight systems with a track record of compliance issues—particularly those sensitive to climate stressors—providing park managers with a data-driven “early warning” system.

title: NPS_PWS
text:
🗺️ Interactive System Explorer
Use the map below to explore public water systems across the National Park network. Filter layers using the top-right control to toggle park boundaries and water system classifications. Hover over any point to view the system name, exact population served, and size classification. Click on any park boundary to identify the official administrative unit name.

# Load the tabular metadata (if needed for other plots, otherwise loaded here)
wsd_sdwa <- read_csv("data/wsd_sdwa_clean.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# 1. Load your local pre-simplified NPS park boundaries
nps_boundaries <- sf::st_read("data/nps_boundaries.geojson", quiet = TRUE) %>% 
  sf::st_transform(crs = 4326) 

# 2. Load the clean spatial data we exported
map_data <- sf::st_read("data/map_data.geojson", quiet = TRUE) %>% 
  sf::st_transform(crs = 4326)

# Define clean labels for the 11 population scales
pop_labels <- c(
  "<=100", "101-500", "501-1,000", "1,001-3,300", "3,301-10,000", 
  "10,001-50,000", "50,001-100,000", "100,001-250,000", 
  "250,001-500,000", "500,001-1,000,000", ">1,000,000"
)

# 3. Add back the hover text dynamically using your existing 'classification' column
map_data <- map_data %>%
  mutate(
    # Re-establish the factor levels so the legend stays in perfect numerical order
    POP_CAT_11_CODE = factor(POP_CAT_11_CODE, levels = as.character(1:11)),
    
    # Create the HTML list column on the fly
    hover_text = lapply(paste0(
      "<strong>System Name:</strong> ", PWS_NAME, "<br/>",
      "<strong>Classification:</strong> ", classification, "<br/>",
      "<strong>Population Served:</strong> ", scales::comma(POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT), "<br/>",
      "<strong>Population Range:</strong> ", POP_CAT_11_DESC
    ), HTML)
  )

# 4. Create palette mapping
pop_palette <- colorFactor(
  palette = "YlOrRd", 
  domain = map_data$POP_CAT_11_CODE
)

# Get unique classifications to build the interactive layers dynamically
classifications <- unique(map_data$classification)

# 5. Initialize the leaflet map
m <- leaflet() %>%
  addProviderTiles(providers$Esri.WorldTopoMap) %>% 
  setView(lng = -98.5795, lat = 39.8283, zoom = 4) %>%
  
  # Add National Park Boundaries
  addPolygons(
    data = nps_boundaries,
    fillColor = "#2a9d8f",             
    fillOpacity = 0.15,                
    color = "#1d3557",                 
    weight = 1.2,                      
    popup = ~UNIT_NAME,                
    group = "Park Boundaries"          
  )

# 6. Dynamically add a separate marker layer for each classification
for (cl in classifications) {
  sub_data <- map_data %>% filter(classification == cl)
  
  m <- m %>% addCircleMarkers(
    data = sub_data,
    lng = ~source_longitude, 
    lat = ~source_latitude,
    radius = 6,                      
    color = "#4a4a4a",               
    fillColor = ~pop_palette(POP_CAT_11_CODE), 
    fillOpacity = 0.8,
    stroke = TRUE,
    weight = 0.8,
    label = ~hover_text,
    labelOptions = labelOptions(
      style = list("font-weight" = "normal", padding = "3px 8px"),
      textsize = "13px",
      direction = "auto"
    ),
    group = cl # Assigns this subset of dots to its own toggle group
  )
}

Warning in RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(max(3, n), palette): n too large, allowed maximum for palette YlOrRd is 9
Returning the palette you asked for with that many colors
Warning in RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(max(3, n), palette): n too large, allowed maximum for palette YlOrRd is 9

Warning in RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(max(3, n), palette): n too large, allowed maximum for palette YlOrRd is 9
Returning the palette you asked for with that many colors
Warning in RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(max(3, n), palette): n too large, allowed maximum for palette YlOrRd is 9
Returning the palette you asked for with that many colors

# 7. Add map styling controls and the interactive legend
m %>%
  addLegend(
    pal = pop_palette, 
    values = map_data$POP_CAT_11_CODE, 
    opacity = 0.8, 
    title = "Population Served",
    labFormat = labelFormat(transform = function(x) pop_labels[as.numeric(x)]),
    position = "bottomright"
  ) %>%
  
  # Interactive Layer Toggles
  addLayersControl(
    overlayGroups = c("Park Boundaries", classifications),
    options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE),
    position = "topright"
  )

title: NPS_PWS
section: 🔮 The Analysis to Come: Developing a System Priority Index
text:
🔮 The Analysis to Come: Developing a System Priority Index
With both infrastructural characteristics (population served, water source, classification) and dynamic human stressors (5-year average visitor footprint) unified in a single dataset, the next phase of this project will build a System Priority Index (SPI).
The SPI will serve as a weighted decision-matrix to rank water systems according to vulnerability and human impact. This predictive model will weight three core dimensions:
Infrastructural Load: The localized population served by the specific system. Environmental Exposure: The physical water source (Surface Water vs. Groundwater), assessing vulnerability to direct climate risks like droughts and surface contamination. Systemic Pressure: The rolling 5-year baseline of overall park visitation, indicating the system’s susceptibility to high-use seasonal spikes.
By mapping this priority index, we aim to provide park managers and sustainability teams with an objective, data-driven framework to prioritize capital improvements, proactive water testing, and targeted infrastructure investments across the national park network.

# 1. Direct URL to the official IRMA Visitor Use Statistics "Main_Data.csv"
# (This contains historical visitation data up through the most recent calendar year)
visitation_url <- "https://irma.nps.gov/DataStore/DownloadFile/756959"

# 2. Read the CSV directly from the web
visitor_data_raw <- read_csv(visitation_url, show_col_types = FALSE)

# 3. Process visitation: Monthly Sums -> Annual Totals -> 5-Year Average
park_visitation_5yr <- visitor_data_raw %>%
  # Filter for the last 5 complete years and the "TV" statistic
  filter(
    Year >= 2021 & Year <= 2025,
    Statistic == "TV"
  ) %>%
  
  # Step A: Sum monthly values to get the total annual visitors for each park and year
  group_by(UnitCode, Year) %>%
  summarize(
    Annual_Visits = sum(Value, na.rm = TRUE), 
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  
  # Step B: Average those annual totals over the 5-year window
  group_by(UnitCode) %>%
  summarize(
    Avg_Annual_Visits_5Yr = mean(Annual_Visits, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) 

# 4. Merge the datasets
wsd_with_5yr_visitors <- wsd_sdwa %>%
  left_join(park_visitation_5yr, by = c("park_unit" = "UnitCode"))

# 5. Save your new master dataset
write_csv(wsd_with_5yr_visitors, "data/wsd_sdwa_visitors.csv")
message("Successfully joined 5-year 'TV' averages and saved to data/wsd_sdwa_visitors.csv")

Successfully joined 5-year 'TV' averages and saved to data/wsd_sdwa_visitors.csv



# 2. Prep the main plotting data (remove NAs and zeros)
viz_data <- wsd_with_5yr_visitors %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT), 
    !is.na(Avg_Annual_Visits_5Yr),
    POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT > 0,  
    Avg_Annual_Visits_5Yr > 0      
  ) %>%
  distinct(pws_number, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  # Calculate a baseline priority score
  mutate(Priority_Score = POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT * Avg_Annual_Visits_5Yr)

# 3. Create a tiny subset containing ONLY the top 10 systems for labeling
# This dramatically reduces ggrepel's calculation load
label_data <- viz_data %>%
  arrange(desc(Priority_Score)) %>%
  slice(1:10)

# 4. Create the Plot
ggplot(viz_data, aes(x = Avg_Annual_Visits_5Yr, y = POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT)) +
  geom_point(
    aes(size = Priority_Score, color = classification), 
    alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  # Log scales
  scale_x_log10(
    labels = comma_format(accuracy = 1, scale = 1/1e6, suffix = "M"), 
    name = "5-Year Average Annual Park Visitation (Log Scale)"
  ) +
  scale_y_log10(
    labels = comma_format(),
    name = "System Population Served (Log Scale)"
  ) +
  # Draw labels (highly simplified)
  geom_text_repel(
    data = label_data,
    aes(label = PWS_NAME),
    size = 2.8,
    max.overlaps = 10,
    color = "#222222",
    fontface = "bold.italic",
    box.padding = 0.4,
    point.padding = 0.3,
    segment.color = "grey50"
  ) +
  # Styling Bubble Size
  scale_size_continuous(
    range = c(2, 10), 
    name = "Priority Score",
    labels = comma_format()
  ) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Classification") +
  theme_minimal() +
  # CRITICAL: Legend fixes to prevent the zero-dimension error
  theme(
    text = element_text(color = "#4a4a4a"),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9, face = "italic"),
    
    # Move the legend to the bottom and shrink the keys to save space
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    legend.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 8.5),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.spacing.y = unit(0.1, "cm")
  ) +
  # Limit the legend rows so it doesn't clip horizontally
  guides(
    color = guide_legend(nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE),
    size = guide_legend(nrow = 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Multidimensional Priority Mapping: Infrastructure Load vs. System Pressure",
    subtitle = "Analysis of unique NPS water systems by localized population and 5-year average annual park visitation."
  )

Warning: ggrepel: 4 unlabeled data points (too many overlaps). Consider
increasing max.overlaps
